title: Commercial Roofing in Lansing Township, MI | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Lansing
metaDescription: Roofing for Eastwood Towne Center's multi-tenant retail and office roofs: tenant HVAC flashing risk, portfolio inspections, and freeze-thaw damage on patched roofs.

html:
Eastwood Towne Center's Roofs Are One Property With a Dozen Different Tenants

Lansing Township's commercial roof stock centers on the Eastwood Towne Center corridor, a lifestyle retail and office development where a single ownership entity often oversees roof systems across multiple buildings occupied by tenants who each have their own opinions about rooftop equipment. That structure creates a specific kind of roofing problem: not one roof, but a portfolio of connected roofs where one tenant's mechanical contractor can create a leak on the roof section next door.

Multi-Tenant Roofs Mean Multiple Points of Failure

When a retail or restaurant tenant brings in their own HVAC contractor to swap a rooftop unit, that contractor is focused on the mechanical connection, not the roof membrane around the new curb. We've walked more than a few roofs in this corridor where a recently replaced unit sits on a curb that was never properly reflashed, and the leak doesn't show up until the next hard rain, sometimes in a neighboring tenant's space rather than the one that had the work done.

For the property manager or ownership group holding the roof warranty and the maintenance responsibility, that's a real exposure. We recommend any tenant-driven rooftop equipment change go through a sign-off step where the roofing contractor of record inspects the flashing before the mechanical contractor leaves the site, and we've built that into maintenance agreements for buildings in this corridor specifically because of how often it gets skipped otherwise.

Office and Retail Roofs, Different Traffic Patterns

The office buildings in this development see roof traffic mostly from mechanical maintenance, on a predictable schedule tied to HVAC service contracts. The retail and restaurant buildings see more ad hoc traffic, satellite dish installs, signage crews, seasonal decoration crews running conduit for lighting, and each one of those visits is a chance for someone without roofing training to walk across a membrane wrong or drop a tool that punctures it. Tracking who's been on a roof and why is part of managing a lifestyle-center property, and it's information we ask for when we're diagnosing a leak that doesn't have an obvious source.

Coordinating Around Retail and Restaurant Hours

A lifestyle center like this doesn't have the dead retail hours a standalone big-box store might have overnight. Restaurants run late, some retail stays open into the evening, and there's regular foot traffic through common areas most of the day. We schedule loud work, membrane removal and mechanical fastening especially, for the hours that actually are quiet for a given building, which sometimes means early morning starts rather than a standard workday.

What We Check on a Multi-Building Roof Portfolio

An inspection across a property like this isn't one roof walk, it's several, and the findings need to roll up into something ownership can actually use to plan a capital budget across the portfolio rather than reacting building by building. The items that consistently need tracking across a multi-tenant retail property include:

  • Flashing condition around every tenant-installed rooftop unit, including additions made after original construction
  • Sealant condition at satellite dish and signage mounts added after original construction
  • Roof age and remaining warranty status by individual building, not the property as a whole
  • Drain and scupper capacity relative to each building's actual roof size
  • Membrane wear near common walkways where maintenance and delivery traffic concentrates

Freeze-Thaw Damage Compounds Faster on Patched Roofs

A roof with a lot of small penetrations added over time doesn't handle a Michigan winter as well as one with fewer interruptions in the field. Every extra curb and mount is another spot for ice to work into a seam during a freeze-thaw cycle. On the Eastwood corridor roofs, where equipment has been added piecemeal for years, we see that compounding effect directly, more penetrations correlating with more winter leak calls, which is part of why we push for the sign-off process on new rooftop work rather than treating it as a minor mechanical detail.

Where a roof has reached the point of diminishing returns on patch repairs, a recover system can consolidate all those old penetration details under a new, properly detailed field membrane rather than continuing to chase leaks one curb at a time.

Questions Lansing Township Property Managers Ask Us

Who's responsible when a tenant's HVAC contractor causes a roof leak?

That depends on the lease and the roof warranty terms, but from a practical standpoint the fastest fix is catching the bad flashing before the mechanical contractor leaves the site. We recommend building that sign-off into tenant improvement policies.

Can you inspect multiple buildings on the same property in one visit?

Yes, and we recommend it for portfolios like Eastwood, because comparing roof age and condition across buildings is how you build a realistic capital plan instead of reacting to each leak separately.

Do you work around restaurant and retail operating hours?

Yes. We schedule the loudest phases of any job for the times a specific building is actually quiet, which we confirm with each tenant before work starts rather than assuming standard business hours.

How much does adding rooftop equipment over the years actually hurt the roof?

Every added penetration is another flashing detail that can fail, and on older roofs with a lot of accumulated equipment we see that add up into more frequent winter leaks. It's a real factor in whether patch repair or a full recover makes sense.

What does a roof condition report look like for a multi-building property?

We break it out building by building with individual age, warranty status, and repair priority, then roll it into a single summary so ownership can see the whole portfolio's risk at once.
